system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 10: Jazz Standard Study #9: All the Things You Are

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 10 — Jazz Standard Study #9: All the Things You Are

_Pages 149-167._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## cycling 4ths importance

> Most notably, it’s a perfect
> study of cycling 4ths, which as we know, is an important movement in
>
> jazz repertoire.

**Significance (p. 149).** Identifies the primary harmonic concept the chapter is built around, giving the learner a framework before any analysis begins.

## AABA form and key centers

> This jazz standard has an
> AABA form and cycles through five different key centers. It is most commonly
> played in the key of concert Ab major. It has a slightly longer 36 bar form as
> opposed to a more standard 32 bar form.

**Significance (p. 149).** States the structural facts a player must know before studying the chord map or attempting to improvise.

## vi chord starts cycling 4ths

> Notice that it starts with the vi chord. This vi chord starts a long cycle
> of 4ths all the way through the Dpmaj7 in bar 5. Everything here is
> diatonically pure to the key center.

**Significance (p. 158).** Explains the functional role of the opening vi chord and establishes that the first eight bars are a clean diatonic cycle of 4ths.

## adding ii before V

> The basic chord change in bar 6 is a simple G7 which acts as a V chord
> moving into Cmaj7, the new | chord. However, sometimes musicians
> will add aii in front of it (Dmin7), making it a short ii-V-I. Where there is
> a V chord, you can almost always add a ii in front of it.

**Significance (p. 158).** Gives an actionable rule—any V chord can be preceded by a ii chord—that applies across all jazz repertoire, not just this tune.

## bars 9-16 key transposition

> What | want you to pay special attention to here is that bars 9-16 are
> literally an exact replica of the first 8 bars of the tune. The only differ-
> ence here is we are starting in a different key. But if you look at the
> roman numeral analysis, everything is exactly the same.

**Significance (p. 159).** Teaches the structural insight that recognizing transposed repetition reduces apparent complexity and aids memorization.

## V chord resolves to new key

> The V chord is always your secret weapon
> for resolving to a new key center.

**Significance (p. 160).** Distills a universal harmonic rule into a memorable principle for navigating key-center changes during improvisation.

## passing diminished connects chromatically

> In this case, the biii diminished isn’t nec-
> essarily replacing anything. Its primary job is to connect the ili chord to
> the ii chord chromatically.

**Significance (p. 161).** Defines the function of the passing diminished chord in this specific context, distinguishing it from its substitution role elsewhere in the book.

## bars 29-32 harmonic summary

> Think of bars 29-32 this way: The IV chord needs to connect to the
> ii chord. There are many different ways to do that, but Jerome Kern
> chose to do it this way. The result is some beautiful harmonic move-
> ment that departs from the cycling 4ths solution of connecting them
> (ii-V’s). The song ends with a simple ii-V-I in the parent key.

**Significance (p. 161).** Frames the non-diatonic passage as a deliberate alternative to cycle-of-4ths motion, giving players a conceptual handle for the hardest section of the form.

## six improvisation tools

> 1. Guide tones. | believe guide tones are helpful to connect chord
> changes together. That’s why I’ve mapped them out for you in
> every lesson.
>
> 2. Use the melody. Not sure which notes to choose? Start with the
> melody, and incorporate it into what you're playing.
>
> 3. Use the chord tones. The root and 5" are up for grabs too. Map
> out all of the chord tones. It can help you identify more note
> choices to work with.
>
> 4. Write a contrafact. Take a step back and slow the improvisa-
> tion process down. Compose your own simple melody so you can
> begin to discover how to turn note choices into musical phrases.
>
> 5. Use motifs. Sometimes creating rhythmic and melodic patterns
> over tricky passages can help you simplify things and develop
> themes.
>
> 6. Write your own licks, or transcribe them. Break the song down
> into smaller parts and compose licks over different chord pro-
> gressions. Even better: learn from the best and grab some licks
> from your favorite jazz musicians. Go straight to the records.

**Significance (p. 162).** Consolidates the book's full improvisation toolkit into one numbered list, making it the go-to reference for how to approach any difficult standard.

## composing solos practice

> My goal when composing solos is to slow the improvisation process
> down and give myself the space to dream up precisely what | would
> like to improvise.

**Significance (p. 163).** States the pedagogical rationale for the composed-solo exercise, framing composition as a deliberate practice method that develops jazz language.

## solo composition instruction

> You don’t have
> to write it down. Just compose it on your instrument and memorize it.

**Significance (p. 163).** Gives a concrete, low-barrier instruction for executing the solo-composition practice without requiring notation skills.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
